steps.text: Determine whether the licensee’s fuel handling procedures are adequate to perform intended functions.
Determine whether fuel is moved and inspected consistent with the requirements of the TS and the licensee’s procedures.
Determine whether fuel handling activities are conducted in accordance with 10 CFR Part 20 and the licensee’s procedures and programs for radiation protection.
Determine whether the licensee satisfied security plan requirements for fuel movement activities.
Determine whether significant fuel movement or inspection problems are identified and resolved in accordance with the licensee’s procedural controls.
Determine whether the licensee was within TS limits and met procedural requirements before resuming normal operation after fuel movement.
Be aware of the facility’s plans and schedules for refueling or other major fuel movement.
Review the safety analysis report (SAR) or as-built facility descriptions, the TS, the licensee’s procedure(s), and the Security Plan for fuel
During the onsite inspection, be alert for significant differences in the SAR, or the TS descriptions of fuel and equipment as compared to the actual fuel and equipment.
Perform design change review and approval in accordance with 10 CFR 50.59 and the licensee’s procedures.
Verify if differences are reflected in the as-built description of the facility.
Refer to ANSI/ANS-15.2 and Regulatory Guide 2.3 for guidance on new, plate-type fuel.
Refer to ANSI/ANS Standards listed in Appendix B to Inspection Procedure 69001 for general guidance.
Refer to Division 2 Regulatory Guides and other listed Regulatory Guides for additional general guidance.
Observe one fuel movement activity.
Verify the final fuel location.
Review respective records.
Discuss with personnel involved in the most recent fuel movement activity.
Verify if the final location of the fuel is consistent with that specified in the licensee's records.
Remove only one fuel bundle at a time.
Inspect the fuel bundle underwater.
Replace the fuel bundle before proceeding.
Inspect only one bundle at a time underwater and replace it before proceeding.
Monitor changes in the level of neutron flux.
Inspect fuel and control rods in accordance with the TS and procedures.
Periodically inspect the fuel and visually determine its integrity.
Check the control rods to determine their capability to continue to function.
Investigate and resolve control rod failures.
Inspect the removed neutron source for leakage paths such as splitting or pitting.
Observe radiological controls during one fuel movement activity.
Review radiation protection records and discuss with responsible personnel for the most recent fuel movement activity.
Monitor or sample for airborne radioactive particulate matter.
Perform grab sampling in accordance with approved licensee procedures.
Monitor exits from controlled areas in accordance with 10 CFR Part 20 and the licensee's procedural requirements.
Shield the detector area or extend the controlled area to a low radiation area.
Perform surveys for contamination in accordance with licensee procedures.
Survey licensee personnel, tools, and equipment for contamination upon exit of the work area.
Check the work exit area for contamination when each person leaves.
Check the work exit area for contamination when each person leaves, and survey licensee personnel, tools, and equipment for contamination upon exit.
Monitor radiation areas in accordance with Subpart F of 10 CFR Part 20, the TS, and the licensee's procedural requirements.
Survey periodically to determine whether actual radiation levels are consistent with area radiation monitors.
Check direct reading dosimeters frequently.
Ensure personnel monitoring devices (film badges, TLDs, direct reading dosimeters, and extremity monitors) are worn correctly and are appropriate for expected radiation fields and the type of work.
Observe security controls during one fuel movement activity.
Review records and hold discussions with responsible personnel for the most recent fuel movement activity.
Observe the resolution of one fuel movement problem, or if direct observation is not possible, review the licensee's logs and records and discuss with responsible personnel.
Verify that the licensee resolved identified problems (e.g., fuel integrity) to meet TS requirements.
Observe one reactor startup after fuel movement, or if direct observation is not possible, review respective licensee logs and records and discuss with responsible personnel.
If core configuration changes occurred, verify calibration of control rods and determination of excess reactivity and shutdown margin.

preconditions.text: If practical, review prior to the onsite inspection.
Significant differences in the SAR or TS descriptions of fuel and equipment compared to actual fuel and equipment are identified.
Direct observation of a fuel movement activity is not possible at the time of inspection
Ensure participants are trained and understand their tasks.
Conduct dry runs, including discussion of actions needed to avert or mitigate accidents, for operations of potential safety concern or of an unusual nature.
An installed neutron source is removed during refueling.
Direct observation is not possible at the time of inspection.
If grab samples are substituting for continuous air monitors to verify compliance with 10 CFR Part 20 and the TS.
If area radiation levels are so high that a difference of about 0.2 millirem per hour (mr/hr) cannot be detected.
If people are continuously entering and exiting the area to observe the work.
If direct observation of security controls is not possible at the time of inspection
Detailed records of the tests and checks were not already covered under the Inspection Procedure on 'Class I Research and Test Reactor Surveillance'.

constraints.text: Under no circumstances is the licensee to adjust schedules for these activities to fit the inspection schedule.
Reference to this guidance is to aid in the technical evaluation of licensee programs and is not to be used as requirements unless the licensee has committed to them in writing.
The use of approved procedures are required in accordance with TS and security plan requirements.
Ensure that the fuel is not damaged, that accidental criticality does not occur, and that radiation and contamination hazards are minimized.
The licensee is required to handle fuel in accordance with the TS and applicable procedures during refueling, fuel shuffling, and fuel inspection.
Participants must be trained and understand their tasks.
Dry runs may be necessary for operations of potential safety concern.
Licensee's fuel handling tools must be consistent with TS requirements and licensee procedures.
Transfer fuel into a core configuration, or to racks or containers designed to prevent accidental criticality.
Calculate the minimum number of fuel bundles for a critical mass with optimum moderation, or separate the bundles using designed fuel racks.
Perform 1/M calculations independently by more than one person when required.
Fuel movement can cause significant radiological hazards. A single fuel element can easily emit radiation at levels above 100 rem per hour at one foot.
Maintain occupational doses as low as reasonably achievable (ALARA).
A Geiger Muller (GM) detector is normally used for exit surveys.
10 CFR Part 20, Subpart F requires surveys and ANSI/ANS 15.11, 1993 provides additional guidance in the section on 'Radioactive Contamination Monitoring'.
Personnel dosimetry is required by 10 CFR 20.1502 and is to be used as specified by the licensee's procedures.
While the reactor is dismantled during fuel movement, the defined security area and patrols must be maintained as specified in the facility security plan.
New fuel is required to be kept in the security area and locked in a fuel vault or secured so that it cannot be carried away by hand.
Spent fuel must be controlled in storage racks in a spent fuel pool or in the reactor pool.
